headline: Scientists Unite Against Nuclear Weapons, 1955
meta_description: In 1955, 18 Nobel laureate scientists signed the Mainau Declaration against nuclear weapons, sparking a global movement.
summary_short: Seventy years ago, some of the world's greatest scientists joined forces to publicly oppose nuclear weapons, catalyzing a historic peace movement.
body:
On this day in 1955, eighteen Nobel Prize-winning scientists made a bold public stand. Led by physicists Otto Hahn and Max Born, they signed the Mainau Declaration, a statement calling for an end to nuclear weapons and the dangers they posed to humanity.

The declaration struck a chord. Within a single year, more than thirty additional scientists—many of them chemists and physicists of equal stature—added their names to the document. These were figures whose work had shaped modern science, and their unified voice carried real weight in global conversations about nuclear policy.

The moment captured something vital: that humanity's brightest minds, who understood the physics of these weapons better than anyone, recognized a moral imperative to warn the world. Their collective courage helped establish that scientists had a responsibility not just to pursue knowledge, but to speak out when that knowledge posed existential risks.
